name: Copper peptides
lead: GHK-Cu is a tripeptide-copper complex with genuine wound-healing and remodelling biology. The cosmetic case is more interesting than most and the formulation pitfalls are worse — copper interacts unpredictably with most other actives, which is why it lives mostly on its own.

whatItIs.body: HK-Cu is a copper-binding tripeptide (glycyl-histidyl-lysine) that occurs naturally in plasma and declines with age. Discovered in the 1970s, it has a respectable wound-healing pedigree: it modulates fibroblast and stem-cell behaviour, supports angiogenesis at injured sites, and influences a long list of skin-relevant genes. The cosmetic translation is real but narrower than the molecular literature suggests — most of the in-vitro effects do not survive the journey to a topical serum.

mechanism.b: Modulates collagen and elastin synthesis at injured tissue. Strongest evidence for surgical and post-procedure recovery.
Supports MMP balance and dermal matrix turnover at sustained use.
Indirect support via fibroblast signalling. Not a direct moisturiser.

evidence.note: The strongest claim. Reproduced across decades of post-surgical and chronic-wound studies.
Modest cosmetic effect at 12+ weeks. Slower than retinoid; pairs with retinoid only via AM/PM split.
Off-label scalp applications show real but modest signal. Not minoxidil.
Well-tolerated when not stacked with conflicting actives.

concentration.b: Where most clinical data lives. Single-active product, dedicated step.
Above 5% rarely improves outcome; vehicle stability matters more.

pairings.note: Direct chelation; both reduced. Use opposite ends of routine if at all.
Redox conflict with copper ion; degrades both. AM C, PM copper peptide minimum.
AM copper peptide, PM retinoid. Same-step layering is wasteful.
Low pH disrupts the copper-peptide complex. Always separate.
On its own, in a dedicated step, in a clean vehicle. The serum it deserves.

products.name: Copper Amino Isolate Serum 3:1:1
products.note: Reference. Two-bottle activation system; the only formulation that takes stability seriously.

products.name: 'Buffet' + Copper Peptides 1%
products.note: Affordable but the multi-peptide vehicle dilutes the copper case.

products.name: Copper Peptide Serum
products.note: The original. Functional, dated formulation, but the dose is honest.

products.name: Hydra-Glow Copper Serum
products.note: Mixed-active product. Copper case gets buried.

faq.q: Why can't I layer it with everything?
faq.a: Copper is a redox-active metal. It chelates niacinamide, oxidises vitamin C, and the peptide complex falls apart at low pH. The molecule is biologically interesting and chemically antisocial.

faq.q: Will it actually firm my skin?
faq.a: Modestly, slowly, with consistent use over 16+ weeks. It is not a substitute for retinoids. It is a credible adjunct for users who want a wound-healing-pedigree active.
Yes. Decades of evidence in surgical and chronic-wound contexts. Whether that translates fully to a cosmetic serum on intact skin is a different question.
Limited topical-specific data. Generally considered cautious-OK at standard cosmetic concentrations; ask your OB if you want a definitive answer.
